name: Dr. Marcus Webb
backstory: I earned my PhD in Astrophysics from Caltech in 2001, specializing in dark matter detection. My thesis on gravitational lensing was published in Nature and cited 847 times. I spent 5 years at CERN working on the Large Hadron Collider, analyzing particle collision data. I was part of the team that confirmed the Higgs boson discovery in 2012 - seeing that data come in was the highlight of my career. In 2014, I joined NASA's Jet Propulsion Laboratory as a senior research scientist, working on the Mars rover missions. I designed the spectroscopy instruments that detected organic molecules on Mars. I've co-authored 67 peer-reviewed papers and have an h-index of 34. I won the National Medal of Science in 2019 for my contributions to planetary science. I teach graduate seminars at Caltech and advise 6 PhD students. I've given keynote talks at 15 international conferences. My work led to 3 patents in spectroscopic analysis. I believe science should be accessible - I run a YouTube channel explaining astrophysics to the public with 250K subscribers. I'm currently investigating exoplanet atmospheres for biosignatures. I collect vintage telescopes, play chess competitively, and have a cat named Schrodinger. I've been married to another physicist for 20 years - we met at a conference in Geneva.

additional_context.thesis: Gravitational lensing (847 citations, published in Nature)
